data.title: NextPower: An Interesting Company With A Fair Bit Of Risk
data.description:
In this article, I'll be taking a look at a relatively new electrical components company that seems to have been raising eyebrows as of late. The company is called NextPower (NXT) and has, since about mid-2025, moved from less than $45/share to above $114/share, where it trades today. The company has a market capitalization of $17B, almost completely lacks debt, and has been able to grow its earnings at remarkable rates in the triple digits.

Bulls are very positive on the business - and many ratings are bullish. The company has "BUY" ratings from SA, from Wall Street, and from Quant, which even puts the company at a "STRONG BUY". I thought it a good idea to check out the company and see what this is about - especially since for about 4 months now, the development has been more "flat" than before.

Does this represent more of a temporary stopping point before new growth and earnings heights, or is the company's story "over" here, and investors should expect normalization of earnings (in which case profit rotation is a good idea).

I've been reading up on NXT for about a week at this point, and i'm prepared to deliver my initial verdict and view on the company's premium as well as on the company's operations and upside.

To those unfamiliar with the company, NXT works with solar power plants. By that I mean that they provide what I would consider a near-vertically integrated platform and service for design, engineering, and supplying of solar trackers. These are basically movers for solar panels, designed to enable the panels to follow the path of the sun. This increases solar panel efficiency, according to the company, by about 20-30% compared to a standard system.

The software uses AI and machine learning, so the company has a correlation or "tilt", if you pardon the pun to this segment as well.

But NXT is expanding, and this is what makes it interesting.

NextPower - Interesting operations, profitable solutions

Far from "only" supplying their moving solutions, the company is moving into structural and electric solutions, wiring, pre-assembled details, metal frames, as well as, recently, robotics that go into automated solar panel cleaning, which has been one of the issues for large-scale solar panels even here in Sweden, where I live.

Of course, aside from everything mentioned, the company also provides service and training for all the products that it offers.

It's fair to say, therefore, that NXT is a platform-oriented solutions provider for enabling "better" solar. I would argue, with the solutions that it offers, it may be a company that could make solar actually viable in the longer-term. This is because the solutions offered by the company are actually required to, over time, have these panels not require constant monitoring and cleaning.

If you follow my work, you'll know that i'm generally not a fan of renewable energy solutions, aside from Hydro. Hydro works perfectly. Wind and Solar have their applications, but the notion that they are currently capable of fully replacing anything legacy is entirely premature.

That being said, they have their uses, and I am willing to follow along if it makes sense. But the consumers, for instance, in Sweden, who put $15,000 solar panels on their roofs, generating the equivalent of $200-$500 worth of electricity per year, that's just a project that has me shaking my head. So it's about the application. If you live in an area that's more favorable for the sun, the calculation is obviously very different.

We start, in this case, by looking at the balance sheet and filings. This is where we find glaring holes in the company's story. The good news?

There are none, or at least very few. The company takes revenues of upwards of $3.6B as of LTM, moves through COGS at 32% (good enough), comes out at 20%+ EBIT margin (very good, really), and moves down to a net margin of over 16%. On the very basic level, this is very good - and the margins have been rising. The company is a profitable business, and as far as I can tell, has been for many years.

This earns a high mark from me.

Furthermore, as mentioned, the company lacks any meaningful debt. It has no long-term debt. It's total debt is $44M, which, together with its cash position, turns it into a net cash company at over $900M LTM.

This is a double-high mark from me.

This doesn't mean the company is perfect. Far from it. As of the most recent filings, the company's Stock-based compensation or SBC, amounts to about 15-20% of its net income.

While the company is inherently profitable enough to make this a non-issue right at this time, this is a metric you need to keep your eyes on, because it has the potential to dilute or distort earnings - it decouples comp from performance, and masks operational cost, given that SBC is considered and deducted as a non-cash expense. So keep that in mind - but as I said, not a big issue at this particular time.

The issues that I see in the company are related to margin compression. Despite rising revenue, the company's net margin is down about 300-400 bps in a year, suggesting costs rising above the rate of revenue - but in that short term, there can be multiple explanations for this. Overall, the company seems to be in a very good position in terms of finances, profit, and generation of cash flows.

This is not where its weaknesses lie. It's weaknesses lie, as I can see it, in the predictability and growth of its business. The company has consequently beaten not only the market but its own estimates. However, the degree at which it has beaten suggests to me that it's in a market that's extremely hard to predict.

Most (90%) of it's revenues are tied to the legacy solar-tracking tech. Furthermore, the company is dependent on quite a low number of customers, and it seems that, based on recent filings, the company is going to grow inorganically rather than organically. This typically means a longer ramp-up for bottom-line profit growth.

Also, one of the big risks in the room is that NXT is dependent entirely (to some extent) on big tax subsidies, such as the IRA 45X. If these are changed, which does not seem impossible under the current US economic picture, then this would affect the company profitability.

As a relatively small player in the electrical space, on a comparison globally, the company is also at the mercy of input and materials pricing, which in turn puts it also at the mercy of competition. For the time being, this seems to be working - but the higher it goes and the more time it takes, the bigger the risks that one of the company's peers is taking a bite out of the company's pie. The company is somewhat smaller than the market cap suggests, with an annual FCF of about $400M - which is by the way still excellent, but not in a place where the company is without issues.

This is a successful growth company that navigates a very tricky environment. By the company's own forecasts, as you can see above, legacy will not represent the largest chunk of new revenue growth going forward. It will be new areas, where the company is neither the market leader nor where it is tested, above all tested on a global scale.

NXT is basically taking its leadership in one core area - positioning - and trying to expand it to other areas. By their own admission, again, the revenue growth will be far lower than it has been in the past. It more than doubled its revenue from 2021-2022 - it will not double its revenues in 4-5 years again.

While the company cites improved and high margin in their new business areas, it's very important to remember that these business areas are neither areas where the company has proven expertise, nor where it is "alone" in doing what it does. That means that I believe there will be a bigger margin pressure here. Clients may decide to go with NXT products for a vertically integrated and complete solution - or they may not.

The current forecast in EPS growth reflects these issues. 2024A AEPS growth was 191%. In 2025 it was over 35%. In 2026, where we had 3Q not long ago, the current estimated AEPS growth is less than 4%.

A bearish view would be here that the growth has stalled. At current estimates, no year in 2027-2029 has a higher single-year AEPS growth than 7-17%. That's less than half of what the company provided in a single year previously.

That is where the challenge lies. The company as reached the apex of its current market, and its expansion plans are not without very different issues and challenges than prior.

So how do we value for that?

NextPower - Valuation requires care

When it comes to valuing the company, I am citing a move from a high-growth scenario to one of limited growth. Usually, in growth, we use something like a sales multiple or market scenario - in slowing sales and market, we use a DCF/2-stage FCFE method, or EBITDA multiple to peers that there are. The company's new segments are far easier to account for than its legacy.

The current analyst modelling has the company trading at about $95-$110/share. S&P Global targets are between $60/share and $145/share, with an average of $125/share. That's an extremely wide range of assumptions, with everything from a complete growth stall and a valuation normalization that amounts to a P/E of a mature industrial cyclical, to one that amounts to a 30x+ P/E growth company.

The industrial average for this segment is roughly 20x P/E. That means the company would be about 20-30% overvalued at 26x P/E, and should trade closer to $80/share. With no debt and over $900M available to the company as well as a significant backlog we may see some time before this lower-growth thesis materializes based on continued AEPS growth. It would be wise to watch not only conversion, but additions to the company's backlog, as well as the margins from the new business and inorganic additions. The net cash works as an argument against the normalization of multiples, for as long as it persists.

But I argue that investors may not be best-served by investing here, even if growth doesn't yet slow down. The company has no dividend, which means that all growth needs to come from capital appreciation. The company trades, over time, at a 20-21x P/E, which ironically is also close to the industry average.

Sans a dividend, the company is not set to impress here, even if it were to continue to grow at this sort of rate of 12%.

You're talking about a return of less than 4.5% per year. This means that in order for you, as the investor, to get better returns, you'd need to either get better growth or to get the market to value the company more highly despite no better growth.

For the latter, I consider that to be entirely unrealistic. For the former, it may be possible, but we've established that even the company itself believes it to grow more slowly. And with a rising amount of metrics like SBC, the company is also in a different "phase" than it has been before.

With regards to the investors that invested in the company earlier on - there is nothing but congratulations from me there.

However, if we're talking about investing in the company now - that is an entirely different "beast". None of the positives, such as the backlog, the cash, or the expansion, comes close to accounting for the counter-arguments of the high valuation (over 26x normalized), potential margin pressures due to expansion, and the entirely different markets of inverters and conversions.

It essentially means that the company is starting to compete with companies like Siemens (SIEGY) and Schneider Electric, something I view as a very poor idea.

This company has done extremely well for itself. That, unfortunately, doesn't mean that it will continue to trade like this, or do as well in the future.

I am not the first analyst to suggest a consideration of taking profits here - but I will point to a firm share price target here. Including consideration for its cash position, I believe the company's lower growth rate going forward justifies at most an industry average multiple. What could cause me to change my view would be a dividend, but there is none. What further causes me to discount it is the rising use of SBC, which dilutes existing shareholders, raises costs during downturns (which the company may experience), and misleads GAAP. I always add back SBC, so for LTM, the company's results to me are actually lower than the company's adjusted reporting. High SBC is acceptable for me when a company is very young and scaling up. That is no longer the case with this one - they're an established market leader seeking to enter traditional markets. I do not view SBC as a positive in this context.

20x P/E is the highest forward target I would accept. That means that I put a PT relative to an upside to that target, and that means my PT for the company comes to about $81/share, for a 15% annualized upside to a 2029E result with a 7-9% AEPS growth rate. The input metrics in this model are incredibly sensitive. If, for instance, we assume that SBC grows until 2029 as it grew the last 3-4 years, then the target becomes $72/share due to how I impact EPS from that SBC (and multiples). This assumption is based on an SBC at, or only at a 5-10% annualized growth rate until 2029E, in which case it would stay within what I consider acceptable in context.

This means that NXT is a "HOLD" for me.

Thesis

NextPower is an interesting industrial/tech company moving into legacy conversion and power inverters for solar. The company has had very impressive rates of historical growth, but is now encountering growth challenges due to its scaling up and moving into other areas where it is not the market leader. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $81/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is not doing.

In my IG, Wolf of Value, i cover industrials similar to ROK that i consider to be more attractive than this one - as well as other interesting investments for both conservative income-oriented investors, as well as more growth-oriented investors.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $81/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: WeShop: Will Need Capital Market Support While Weathering Tough Competition
data.description:
WeShop Holdings Limited (WSHP) is a bit of a meme stock, and its gimmick, which has given it a bit of traction, is that as a loyalty programme, instead of giving some redeemable points for a future purchase or something to that effect, there are shares reserved that can be redeemed by users of the platform with their loyalty points. The cost of these contingent shares goes into the COGS as they are granted for free to users. The rate of loyalty points granted for a transaction will depend on the retailer and the listing but is communicated very clearly in advance of purchase. The shares destined to users for the shareback have already been issued, and there isn't a dilution risk imminently associated with that. However, they have 102k GBP in cash as of June. They are not at all profitable (perhaps yet), and they did a direct listing to our understanding and therefore wouldn't have raised money since then. But the 6-month cash burn was almost 2 million GBP, so we expect to see some action to shore up funds very soon, particularly as the business is transitioning from its pilot to its more all-out commercial phase, including its US expansion, with the direct listing now concluded. This is also why we cannot usefully comment further on cash burn and runways; available data is also quite stale from the prospectus. The main thing is that the cash position is small relative to the last recorded cash burn figure.

But beyond dilution from operations on top of the 35% or so from outstanding options and convertibles, we see considerable competitive challenges with other ecommerce platforms and search engines leveraging LLM and other LLM shopping tools. On valuation, ignoring for the moment dilutions that will come at the cost of today's shareholders, P/S is around 70x using the FY 2024 data, which is the last FY data available and represents a year when the pilot was underway. A combination of high valuation and uncertain competitive capacity we pass, particularly on limited cash position and cash burning, as this is a very risky stock with total capital impairment risk to be considered. It doesn't even have the benefit of at least trying to ride the AI hype or offer something in the way of LLM-prompted shopping referrals. It's just a sort of social media app with a feed and associated links to products found in pictures.

The Considerations

The exact mechanism is that the user gets an effective cash back on the WeShop commission at a fixed rate through being granted WePoints, which reflect shares, and the amounts of WePoints granted depend on how many shares the cash back can convert into. So if the stock price rises, the rate of drawdown on the reserved shares in the quantity of 12,500,000 will slow, meaning they won't have to issue more shares to keep the shareback gimmick going. There are 23 million shares outstanding, including the 12,500,000 class B shares reserved for giving to users in the shareback, and they will immediately convert into a class A share once given.

Up to 12,500,000 WePoints will be issued to exchange into up to 12,500,000 Class A ordinary shares. 12,500,000 Class B Ordinary Shares were approved to be deposited into the WeShop Community Trust. These Class B ordinary shares will be held for the Shareback Plan. Upon redemption of a WePoint, a Class B ordinary share will automatically convert into a Class A ordinary share and be delivered to the User redeeming a WePoint.

As of the date of this prospectus, we have 23,437,064 ordinary shares outstanding (consisting of 10,937,064 Class A ordinary shares and 12,500,000 Class B ordinary shares).

Prospectus

Ultimately, the shareback is just a slight variation on an age-old theme of a loyalty programme, but instead of coming out of WeShop's take on GMV, it's going to cost shareholders in the form of dilution, though it has already occurred and is in the current share count.

However, we note the following:

As of June 30, 2025 and December 31, 2024, there were a total of 7,944,346 and 7,896,831 Contingent Shares outstanding, respectively.

What we understand by this is that quite a lot of the reserved shares have already been accounted for by transactions that have occurred and will draw down the reserved shares totaling 12,500,000 in the WeShop Community Trust. Supposing an average 20% shareback rate and 10% commission, over $1.2 billion GMV will need to occur before further dilutions are needed after drawing down on the shares remaining in the trust beyond the contingent share figures. That could take years, given the pilot period GMV was around $146 million and current share prices, which may not hold due to the high valuation.

We understand that there are 5,250,001 shares that are going to be registered and issued as a consequence of performance incentive grants. That is almost a 25% dilution that has begun as of the direct listing.

There are convertibles to consider, but we can figure them as debt for now instead of dealing with the conversion considerations. The thesis isn't that sensitive to this issue.

The notes convert at a price of the shares calculated to a pre-money valuation of the Company of £58,000,000 divided by the number of shares in issue at the conversion date. [a little over ~2 million shares or 10% dilution]

Prospectus

Bottom Line

Our issue with WSHP is that there are major competitive risks to its app, which we should explain is basically a social media where all the feeds are influencers posing with products that can be bought from partnered retailers - WSHP has been pretty good with getting quite a few retailers on board. Other users can also share pics where links will be present to retail items in the picture. Retailers can also pay to have their products be more prominent in feeds.

But the competitive risks are that, firstly, there is e-commerce by the retailers themselves to deal with, as well as other e-commerce platforms that shoppers can go to, like eBay, Amazon, etc. Then, perhaps most concerningly, there are the search engines, which also deal with trying to drive purchase traffic, and those search engines are likely finding novel ways to use LLMs to do that as well - so not just the search engines but also the LLM query platforms. We can't see why WeShop would stand out in the pretty mature world of e-commerce, other than with its share buyback, which, while meme-worthy, smells a little like the pay-to-earn concepts that tried to drive platform adoption related to blockchain. There are considerable competitive risks.

We are not convinced by the 70x P/S valuation either using the FY 2024 data, which represents a full year of the piloted app and model, especially given the balance sheet position and the dependence on achieving scale for profitability and growing into that valuation despite the challenging competitive environment. We use 2024 data because that was a full year of the piloted model, and since then revenues have considerably fallen, with sales and marketing also dialed back considerably. We're going to be charitable and say the declines are because of the end of the pilot and could return to 2024 levels once the model resumes fully.

They were burning cash and had limited cash relative to that burn as of the latest capitalisation figures in mid-year 2025, and the IPO was a direct listing that hasn't injected cash, to our understanding. Therefore, in addition to incentive awards and convertibles, which cap upside with around 35% dilution, there is dilution from the need to tap equity markets, most likely to finance cash-burning operations at the early stages of the company.

data.title: Commodities: Oil Trades Lower On De-Escalation Hopes
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy: US oil exports jump to record levels

The oil market continues to edge lower amid hopes that the US and Iran extend their ceasefire by another 2 weeks, along with a potential resumption in talks to bring an end to the war. However, the physical market is becoming tighter every day that passes without a restart of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

After taking into consideration pipeline diversions and the trickle of tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, we estimate that roughly 13m b/d has been disrupted. But with the US blockade, this number could creep higher.

The divergence between the futures and physical markets is clear: dated Brent traded around $117/bbl, while front-month Brent futures settled a little below $95/bbl yesterday. The key upside risk for the market is that peace talks between the US and Iran break down. This isn’t an unrealistic scenario, given that US and Iranian demands remain fairly wide apart.

The latest data from the Energy Information Administration (EIA) shows that US crude oil exports jumped by 1.08m b/d week-on-week to 5.23m b/d - the highest volume since September 2025. However, total oil and refined product exports surged by 1.03 m b/d WoW, hitting a record 12.74 m b/d.

These stronger US exports reflect buyers turning to other markets for supply amid disruptions in the Middle East. Despite strong exports, US crude oil inventories declined only marginally over the week, falling by 913k barrels. Refined product stocks saw more meaningful declines, with gasoline and distillate inventories falling by 6.33m barrels and 3.12m barrels, respectively.

With buyers shifting toward US barrels, the domestic market is set to tighten as long as Middle East disruptions persist, likely prompting a supply response from US producers. US drilling activity, however, has barely moved since the start of the conflict.

According to Baker Hughes data, the US oil rig count stood at 411 last week, up from 407 prior to the war. The lack of drilling activity also ties in with the EIA’s domestic crude oil production forecasts, which suggest little change in output this year. If we see a pickup in US drilling activity, it would have a more meaningful impact on oil output over 2027.

European gas prices continue to edge lower amid hopes of de-escalation in the Middle East, while EU gas storage levels move closer to 30% full as the region moves deeper into the injection season. High LNG send-outs in Europe have kept the market comfortable.

Investment funds also reduced their net long in TTF by 37.4 TWh over the last week to 271.8 TWh. Clearly, the longer disruptions in the Middle East persist, the more competition we’ll see from Asia as buyers seek alternative supplies. JKM continues to trade at a premium to TTF, so we should be seeing cargoes being redirected towards Asia.

Metals: Copper trades near one-month high on Iran optimism

Copper rose to around a one‑month high this week, with broader gains across industrial metals as markets priced in easing macro risks. Optimism that the US and Iran could restart talks helped unwind some of the recent pressure from fears of higher energy costs and weaker growth.

However, the market remains highly headline‑driven. Any escalation in the conflict, renewed spikes in energy prices or signs of softer demand could quickly reverse sentiment. In a de‑escalation scenario, copper would likely outperform, supported by expectations of eventual rate cuts, a weaker dollar and a broader improvement in risk appetite.

Downstream supply risks are also gaining attention. Sulfuric acid is emerging as a bottleneck for SX‑EW copper output, with around half of seaborne sulfur transiting the Strait of Hormuz.

China’s sulfuric acid prices have risen around 90% since the start of the Iran war. Export bans introduced following the Hormuz shipping halt are raising disruption risks to acid-dependent supply in Chile, Peru and the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

Positioning data points to improved sentiment. Speculators increased their bullish LME copper bets by 2,329 net‑long positions to 48,225, the most bullish stance in more than three months, according to weekly futures and options data.

In precious metals, gold remains supported amid renewed optimism around de‑escalation. The pullback in oil prices is easing some of the inflation concerns that weighed on prices earlier in the conflict. The move reflects a broader shift in market focus.

While gold initially fell sharply as liquidity pressures forced selling, losses have since partially recovered as growth concerns have reemerged and price swings have become less extreme. While higher real rates, a firmer dollar, and profit-taking could weigh on near-term price action, recent pullbacks suggest underlying demand remains resilient.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/16/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Paul Mueller: Strong Q4 Results, Yet The Upside May Not Be Fully Priced In
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Note: This is an update to my previous article.

Paul Mueller (MUEL) had a record fourth quarter with sales growth of 30% and a 59% year-over-year increase in backlog. Also, capital allocation remains shareholder-friendly while the company seeks to capitalize on growth opportunities through a new manufacturing facility.

All of this, combined with an attractive current valuation, leads me to maintain my buy rating and even slightly improve my outlook for the company.

Record Backlog and High Growth

It's been over six months since my last coverage of Paul Mueller. Back then, I commented that the company was becoming an unexpected beneficiary of data centers, but that at the same time, his legacy business was solid and this new market was merely optional. Well, fiscal year 2025 has ended, and it's worth reviewing how that thesis has progressed.

In my previous valuation model, I estimated sales of $268 million for 2025, but I was quite far off the mark, and sales closed at $287 million, representing 15% year-over-year growth thanks to an extraordinary Q4 2025 with 33% growth. This growth wasn't limited to the top line, by the way. Operating income also grew 18% year-over-year, and net margins reached 12% at year-end (pretty much in line with my previous estimate), but Q4 was again stellar, with net margins closing at 14%.

Fourth quarter 2024 2025 Change
Revenue $70.5M $94.1M 33.6%
EBIT $13.6M $17.0M 25.2%
EPS $11.9 $14.7 23.9%

Author's compilation

What strikes me most is that it wasn't just a solid quarter, and rather there are signs that this trend could continue. The backlog reached $243.6 million, up 59% year-over-year, suggesting that demand isn't only holding steady but even accelerating in a way.

Something worth noting is that I previously mentioned the company was entering the data center market through its chillers, heat exchangers, and water storage tanks. However, there's no evidence that this data center exposure is reflected in the positive backlog figures or overall growth. I like this because it implies that the core business is clearly generating the most impact, and any data center-related demand is purely additional and complementary. Imagine being able to say that a growing industry is purely optional for you while growing your backlog to record numbers.

Capital Allocation

The company continues to generate significant cash from its operations, and cash & short-term investments have reached $50 million while having virtually no debt. In 2025, the company allocated $10 million to continue paying down its small debt and almost $17 million to share buybacks, which reduced the share count by 7%. They are also paying a rather modest dividend of $1.20 annually, or a dividend yield of 0.24%.

These are all ways to return value to shareholders, and I appreciate that dividends are among the last capital allocation options, as they tend to be less tax efficient in the United States.

I also want to highlight the capital expenditures, which increased from $17.3 million to $34.4 million. This investment is intended for new facilities and more production capacity through a new 100,000-square-foot manufacturing facility, a project that began in December and is expected to be operational by mid-year. This seems to me a clear indication that the company is positioning itself to pursue growth opportunities, whether in data centers or in its existing markets.

So, it appears that on the one hand, management has confidence in the business and its growth potential, but on the other hand, they remain very focused on shareholders and are looking to deliver value. A very well-planned capital allocation but quite unusual.

Valuation

Previously, it turned out well to start with a conservative valuation and let Paul Mueller surprise me, so I want to maintain this approach while acknowledging the reality of accelerating growth and a backlog reflecting high demand. So, the only changes I'll make to my previous valuation model are a 10% sales growth annually and 0.9 million outstanding shares, since the previous model assumed 0.65 million due to the tender offer, but if you look at the diluted shares on a quarterly basis, the share count again reflects 0.9 million shares outstanding.

Based on these assumptions, the company should be earning $65 in EPS within five years, assuming the share count drops to 0.85 million (around 1% annually). With a multiple of 15x, that would translate to a share price of nearly $980, or double the current price. A reasonable return for a company with good capital allocation and accelerating growth.

Risks

One risk I'm paying particular attention to, and which I didn't mention in my previous coverage, is customer concentration. As of the end of 2025, one industrial client accounted for 23% of sales and another for 9%. Losing or delaying a contract with these clients could have a significant impact on results, especially now that an increasing proportion of revenue is tied to larger and more complex projects.

Another aspect to consider is that Paul Mueller is investing heavily in new capacity. If demand doesn't materialize as expected or if projects experience delays or cost overruns, margins could be affected. So far, execution has been good, but we can never let our guard down.

The Bottom Line

This past quarter was strong and marked a very good year for the company. Also, the significant increase in the backlog makes me think that we could expect more growth in the next one or two years, something I hadn't considered in my previous analysis.

While the capital allocation has been good so far for shareholders, I can't help but think that the share buyback could have a negative effect on high-capital investors, as it means the stock becomes more illiquid and it will be difficult to buy or sell. If this isn't a risk for you, then the company could be an attractive alternative for any portfolio at the current price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amdocs Shows Strong Cash Flow At A Cheap Price
data.description:
Amdocs (DOX) looks like a solid buy here. The company’s core business just keeps chugging along, free cash flow is strong, and they’re steady about returning capital to shareholders. Shares trade at a forward valuation that doesn’t assume much growth, so there’s some cushion if the economy or the telecom sector gets bumpy. The stock dropped about 23% over the past year and is now trading near its 52-week low, even though management reiterated fiscal 2026 free cash flow guidance of $710 million to $730 million and reported a $4.25 billion 12-month backlog. Now, with the price reset, the risk/reward looks better for anyone buying in today, as long as you’re realistic about what could go right and what could go sideways depending on the cycle.

Amdocs is right in the middle of recurring telecom services, digital upgrades, and big new AI projects. Managed services is their anchor, $746 million in Q1, making up 65% of revenue, which lines up with recent quarters. Renewal rates are high. They signed new multi-year deals with Vodafone Germany, T-Mobile, and Telefonica Germany. These contracts beef up the $4.25 billion 12-month backlog reported at Q1, up 2.7% from last year. That’s a lot of revenue already locked in, even though telecom companies are still tight with capital spending.

MATRIXX and Europe

They picked up MATRIXX in December, and the smaller Profinit deal is also adding a bit of growth on top of the core business. About half of the company’s projected 1.5% to 5.5% revenue growth for fiscal 2026 comes from these acquisitions. MATRIXX brings in new charging, rating, billing, and monetization technology that complements Amdocs' broader product portfolio. In Q1, the company saw European revenue grow 17% year-over-year, plus some improvement from other international markets.

Macro

DOX operates in a pretty tangled environment right now. Telecom companies are still feeling the pressure from bigger economic and political uncertainty. Amdocs spelled this out directly and pointed out that telcos are still touchy about spending, especially since tech keeps changing fast. Currency swings are still a thing too; for 2026, they’re only penciling in a 0.5% bump from foreign exchange. But some of the basics look good for Amdocs; telecom firms are still racing to digitize, and there’s a bigger need for platforms that can scale up AI-driven operations, as long as customers actually move forward with adoption. The MATRIXX deal was already baked into their numbers. They also see more upside if they can make money off GenAI and push deeper into the cloud, though those haven’t shown up fully in the numbers yet. Managed services and cloud revenue are both growing at a double-digit pace, while Europe revenue grew 17% year-over-year and managed services remained about 65% of total revenue, especially since U.S. telcos are consolidating and feeling squeezed on costs. That’s pushed Amdocs to chase more business in Europe, the Middle East, and Asia-Pacific.

Cash Flow

The numbers tell most of the story. Q1 free cash flow, before any restructuring, hit $237 million, which is already about a third of their full-year target range of $710 to $730 million. That’s a 10% free cash flow yield based on current market value, and the company is turning about 90% of earnings into cash. The dividend sits at about 3.5%; they just approved $0.569 per share for the last quarter, up from $0.527 a year back. The payout keeps rising, helped by ongoing stock buybacks. There’s still $840 million left authorized for buybacks at the end of 2025. Amdocs says it’s sticking with its forecast for non-GAAP EPS growth between 4 and 8% in 2026 and operating margins between 21.3% and 21.9%, with the middle of that range about 20 basis points higher than last year. The company is trying to keep margins steady by automating more and controlling costs, but at the same time, it’s bumping up what it spends on R&D and sales. Management put it like this on their last call: “Our profitability outlook reflects an intentional decision to accelerate our R&D, sales, and marketing investments with respect to generative AI and our next-gen agentic operating system while balancing this with ongoing cost and efficiency gains.”

Valuation

Forward P/E is under 9, and EV/EBITDA is around 7.6, which is a pretty muted setup for a business with Amdocs cash flow profile. For 2026, consensus revenue is about $4.69 billion, and management is guiding to operating margins in the 21.3% to 21.9% range. If Amdocs lands somewhere around the midpoint and you apply a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple, which is more in line with its longer-term trading range, a fair value in the mid-$80s to mid-$90s per share looks reasonable. Against a current share price of $64.98, that leaves meaningful upside if execution holds.

Sentiment shows people aren’t buying in. Shares are down almost 23% over six months. While DOX reported Q1 revenue slightly above the midpoint of guidance and Q1 non-GAAP EPS above the guidance range while reiterating full-year guidance. Q1’s $1.81 non-GAAP EPS beat the mark, and revenue topped $1.16 billion, both ahead of what Wall Street forecasted.

Risks

The main worry is that telecom stops spending on IT, or the company loses out if newer, cloud-native, or AI-focused players win over customers faster. Their long-term contracts and backlog should keep cash flow solid through 2026 and 2027, but if GenAI doesn’t bring in as much money as hoped. It’s got $248 million in cash and $780 million in debt, with a $130 million revolver draw used to help fund the roughly $197 million MATRIXX acquisition, so the balance sheet looks strong enough that this isn’t a make-or-break risk. T-Mobile makes up a big chunk of revenue, which is a weak spot if U.S. telcos cut their budgets again. Currency swings aren’t small, but they’re hedged, with only a 20 basis point tweak expected in the full-year FX benefit.

What to Watch

The outlook is pretty strong, assuming deals tied to digital transformation and AI don’t get pushed back. In the short run, showing that backlog turns into revenue, keeping EBIT margins over 20%, and locking in early GenAI wins in places already announced would push consensus estimates up. Key things to watch: whether revenue picks up in Rest of World and EMEA, cloud migration starts to accelerate, or changes in restructuring or R&D spending threaten cash flow. On the flip side, if the backlog shrinks, EMEA revenue slides, or T-Mobile deals get weaker, the Buy case falls apart.

Bottom line: Amdocs has one of the strongest risk/reward setups in its space, steady fundamentals, big capital returns, and a real shot at mid-single-digit earnings growth, all at lower-than-normal multiples. Unless there’s clear evidence of revenue falling apart or margins dropping, this gets a Buy.

data.title: COPEL: Risk-Return Ratio Is Still Attractive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on Copel (ELPC) shares. My coverage of the company's shares began in September 2024, and my last article was published on May 26, 2025. My intention in this article is to analyze the investment case again and the arguments of skeptical analysts with the company's investment thesis.

Copel

Copel is one of the largest companies in the Brazilian electricity sector. The company was founded in 1954 and operates in an integrated manner in the segments of generation, transmission, distribution, and commercialization of electrical energy.

This company serves more than 5 million consumer units in 395 municipalities. Its portfolio consists of 49 own plants (hydroelectric and wind). The company was a state-owned company in the Brazilian state of Paraná and was privatized in 2023.

Revisiting My Investment Thesis

I've been following the company's investment thesis for a long time, and here in SA since 2024. In all articles I always recommended buying the shares because the thesis was very clear: privatization would bring great efficiency gains to a company with cheap multiples, not to mention its solid operation for decades and long-term relationships with suppliers.

The company is diversified through operations in the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of energy. Copel has robust cash generation, profitable assets, and comfortable liquidity, not to mention a low-risk business profile.

But since its privatization, Copel has been advancing in its restructuring in search of greater operational efficiency with successful divestments in non-strategic assets in line with the objective of focusing on the electricity business (core business) and maintaining a 100% renewable matrix.

Recent examples of this strategy being followed to the letter were its latest divestments. These improvements appear in the share price, which we will see below, but also in the results, which we will see in the next chapter. The share price has practically doubled since my first article was published less than two years ago.

Latest earnings results

The company reported its latest results on February 26th, so I will not go into detail, as the market has already fully priced in the results, but SA shows the results in a way that makes the analysis very simple and assertive.

The company surprised the market in the last quarter. Net revenue and EPS came in above market expectations.

Reader, now see with your own eyes the company's latest quarterly results:

The company has simply surpassed high market expectations in every quarter since the beginning of 2022. So far I remain extremely optimistic about the company's investment thesis; however, let's get to the culmination of this article.

Valuation

Valuation is the argument most used by analysts who present a skeptical view of the company. These analysts indicate that the current valuation has already priced in much of the growth. I disagree with this view, and I will show my arguments.

When you analyze the company based on its historical EV/EBITDA multiple, it really gives the impression that the company is expensive.

But it is important to remember that the company has never reported such good results, and this needs to be taken into account. This case is very similar to AXIA (NYSE:AXIA) (formerly Eletrobras), which was also privatized and whose multiple reached the highest value in its history.

The multiple will rise as the company improves its results. Not to mention that Copel doesn't deal with the same risks as Eletrobras; after all, Eletrobras was a state-owned company at the federal level, and Copel was a state-owned company at the state level.

In my opinion, the company could undergo a re-rating, and seek the multiples of its global peers. As we can see above, the company is traded at 9.6x EBITDA, and to reach the average for its sector, its valuation could reach 11.78x EBITDA, an upside of 22.7% that corroborates my recommendation to buy the shares.

Potential Threats To Thesis

There are risks inherent to the business, such as geographic concentration and economic dependence on the state of Paraná. Not to mention that the business is subject to regulations, and tariff approval, and there are risks of executing investment projects with lower returns than expected.

As for more specific risks, curtailment has been a major challenge for renewable energy companies. These are cuts in energy generation, imposed by the National Electric System Operator, when the electrical system is unable to absorb all the energy produced.

The Bottom Line

Sometimes investors are faced with profits on their investments and quickly want to find reasons to put the profits in their pockets. Thus, it does not deal with regret if the shares fall, but I recommend that investors analyze Copel's case twice.

Although the multiple has risen significantly, the company is at the best operational moment in its history. The divestment policy and focus on core business has not yet shown all possible results, and investors who sell now may deal with the pain of not having made more profit due to their impatience.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy Copel shares. The strong appreciation of the shares and the high multiple cannot be arguments for selling the shares; it is necessary for investors to see the situation more comprehensively, and in this case, the risk-return ratio is still attractive.

data.title: AeroVironment: Far From A High Flier In A Dynamic Environment
data.description:
Shares of AeroVironment (AVAV) have seen dismal returns as of late, down 30% since early February, while the world feels very uncertain here, with many geopolitical events happening across the globe.

While the external environment should be supportive for AeroVironment, shares have been falling relentlessly recently. This comes amidst volatile results, but likely also fears about competition being on the increase.

While shares have certainly become a lot cheaper recently, traditional earnings metrics show little support amidst thin margins, making me still very reserved about the share here.

Other interesting setbacks and related stories are discussed at Value In Corporate Events.

Eventful Times

Since early February, many developments have been taking place for AeroVironment. Early in April, AeroVironment announced a 3-year $25 million contract from the US Air Force, this really being a bolt-on order.

The order follows a bolt-on deal in mid-March, when the company announced the purchase of Empirical Systems Aerospace, a provider of unmanned aircraft systems and advanced air mobility platforms. The deal values the business at $200 million, comprised of $160 million in stock and the remainder in cash. The transaction is seen as adjusted EBITDA accretive in year one, but apart from this statement, no financial details have been announced.

The California-based business has a combined 85,000 square feet of design & prototyping as well as a manufacturing facility, with multiple other facilities used in the region. The company praises the culture of technical rigor and rapid innovation to develop mission-critical solutions to the US Department of War's requirements.

Mixed Results

The purchase of Empirical followed the release of the third-quarter results early in March. Third-quarter sales rose some 144% to $408 million, driven by the contribution of the $4.1 billion purchase of BlueHalo in May of 2025. So far so good, with operating losses reported at $179 million, largely driven by a $151 million goodwill impairment charge. Adjusted for that, losses were reported as well, with results being impacted by amortization charges, among others.

Adjusted earnings were reported at $0.64 per share, that number doubling from the year before, while year-to-date adjusted earnings of $1.42 per share lag by a quarter per share compared to the year before.

A $400 stock in January has come down to $195 per share, being cut in half. With nearly 50 million shares outstanding, the market value stands just shy of $10 billion, with the entire valuation standing around $10 billion, given a minimal net debt load, also following the latest deal. With revenues trending around $1.6 billion each year, the company trades around 6 times sales. As realistic earnings this year are seen just around $3 per share, anticipating a strong end to the year, earnings multiples are very demanding, as margins are minimal here.

The reality is that the picture is quite volatile and difficult to read into. Third-quarter sales of $408 million were down substantially from a $472 million number in the second quarter and a $455 million sales number in the first quarter, although earnings this quarter were quite solid.

The company sees sales for the year at a midpoint of $1.90 billion, with adjusted EBITDA seen around $275 million and earnings between $2.75 and $3.10 per share. This suggests that the fourth quarter is expected to be much stronger here, as there appear to be some seasonal elements in the business as well. Note that the full-year sales guidance has been cut by $75 million, with earnings guidance revised much lower, after the previous guidance called for earnings between $3.40 and $3.55 per share.

And Now?

The setback in the share price came amidst a softer performance, although valuations have come down more as well. EBITDA multiples are seen down to the mid-thirties. Of course, some smaller M&A and orders are reported meanwhile, but they are not going to change the needle here. Besides the orders mentioned before, AeroVironment furthermore was awarded a $97 million three-year order by the US Army in early March and a larger $186 million order by the end of February.

All of this looks relatively well-balanced, yet expectation keeps going lower (albeit that valuations were quite lofty to start with). This has surprised many investors and commentators, certainly as the conflict in Iran has emerged simultaneously, and drones play a key role in this conflict as well.

It is evident that in any conflicts nowadays, drones play a key part. It is not just the hardware, but notably the software as well, with different tactics needed for both offensive and defensive strategies. However, much innovation is taking place here, and frankly, it seems that countries like Ukraine and others are leading not just in innovation but also in price-efficient practices here.

So, ironically, the sector might benefit on aggregate from increased uncertainty, but that does not mean that AeroVironment and some of its peers are automatic winners here. Furthermore, as reports indicate, the US is focusing on the usage and development of cheaper "LUCAS" drones, and reports indicate that Ukraine might even help the US military here with its technologies.

A Final World

While I am appealed to the US status and the track record of AeroVironment is solid, the reality is that I still have reservations, even as shares have come down quite a bit more recently.

This is despite the political uncertainty, a long-term track record, US status, Golden Dome plans, etc. The reality is that growth is not that impressive, margins are relatively low, results are volatile, expectations are high, and competition is not just fierce; it is rapidly evolving as well.

All this simply makes me a bit cautious and raises the bar to get involved, as I see no compelling reason to buy into the dip just yet here.

data.title: JLS: Mortgages Are Attractive
data.description:
Thesis

Nuveen Mortgage and Income Fund (JLS) is a fixed-income closed-end fund from the Nuveen family. As per its objective, the CEF:

invests at least 80% of its Assets in mortgage related assets including mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and other income producing securities including asset-backed securities (ABS), bonds, debt securities and other similar instruments. The Fund invests at least 50% of its Managed Assets in MBS, including residential MBS and commercial MBS, and up to 50% in non-mortgage related ABS

The CEF has an RMBS focus and has been in the market for a long time, going through several macro cycles. With rates having moved up due to the Iran war and a significant shift up in the yield curve, we are going to visit JLS and articulate why this CEF is attractive.

RMBS-Focused Closed-End Fund

The CEF is overweight RMBS bonds, although it does have a multi-asset approach to its build. While the fund has a poor reporting structure, one can download the Excel file with the holdings and do a proper parsing. The fact sheet segmentation of the holdings is very poor:

What are we supposed to get from the above table as investors? We downloaded the holdings in Excel and did a parsing ourselves and found the following:

CMOs are 33% of the fund, while CMBS bonds are 35% and plain ABS securities are 27%. The name is active; thus, the above percentages can change as the managers see fit.

From a ratings perspective, the CEF has a mix of investment-grade and junk assets:

Roughly 25% of the holdings are investment grade, while roughly 40% are in the junk section, although most of the junk bonds are BB rated. Please note there is a large not-rated bucket, with this being usual for many MBS bonds.

While the stated portfolio weighted average maturity sits at 17.4 years, the stated duration is only 1.3 years because of the prepayment feature for most MBS bonds. Investors should care about duration only here, and the duration is very short. The fund also comes with leverage, which sits at 22%.

So we have a multi-asset CEF with a fairly low duration that is overweight RMBS and CMBS bonds.

Rates Have Moved Higher This Year

Given the ongoing conflict with Iran, oil prices have spiked, bringing back inflation fears. As a result of potential inflation, rates have shifted higher since the start of the year:

The red line in the graph above is the yield curve at the beginning of the year, while the blue line is the curve as of April 14, 2026. As we can see, the blue line has shifted higher, with intermediate portions of the curve up from 20 to 30 bps (looking at 3- and 5-year maturities). This is the direct result of market participants pricing out Fed cuts this year as a result of potential inflationary pressures.

What does it all mean? Well, we have witnessed a violent move up in rates on the back of a resetting of inflation expectations. If the Iran conflict gets resolved soon and we get a flow back in oil and lower prices, the worst-case scenario might not materialize; thus, rates might be too high currently. In fact, as per BlackRock, after the recent move higher in rates, investment-grade-based rate products offer an attractive risk-reward for a portfolio:

For example, if one bought US IG 3Y (US investment grade paper with a 3-year maturity) today at 4.57% yield, rates would need to rise more than 320 bps over the next year for returns to turn negative. On the other hand, if rates fall 50 basis points over the next 1 year, one would make a return of 5.4%. The chart is endemic of the current asymmetry present in the high-grade fixed-income markets, MBS included, where we are towards the top in rates with healthy income generated by the asset class.

CEF Performance

The fund has done very well in the past three years, boosting asset class returns via its leverage profile:

We are comparing JLS here versus the unleveraged iShares MBS ETF (MBB), the leveraged agency play BlackRock Income Trust (BKT), and the high-yield CEF Credit Suisse Asset Management Income Fund (CIK).

We can see JLS outperforming the cohort in the respective period, although for a long period of time CIK was the better fund. Plain vanilla MBS play MBB is barely positive from a total return perspective in the period, while BKT is a disaster with a negative performance. BKT has had issues because of the longer duration profile it runs.

JLS combines good risk/reward via the chosen classes in the RMBS space, going for shorter duration mezz bonds that provide for a high but also low duration profile. And the results speak for themselves.

Premium/Discount to NAV

The market has recognized the outstanding job done by the CEF managers and only trades the name at a slight discount to NAV:

From this metric perspective, we are smack in the middle of the historic range for the CEF. Do note the extremes in discount after the 2022 rates sell-off. We consider the current discount to be fair given the asset class and recent rates sell-off.

Distribution Composition

While the fund has a 9.9% distribution rate, it is not entirely covered by the underlying cash flows. From a recent Section 19a notice, we can see the coverage ratio sitting at roughly 70%:

The 'ROC' column is return of capital and purely represents the percent of the distribution that is investor principal coming back to them rather than cash flow-based fund distribution. While on the higher side, the ROC figure is not egregious, and we are comfortable with what JLS is doing here. The fund has demonstrated its ability to generate value and NAV long term via its trading acumen and portfolio build.

Conclusion

JLS is a fixed-income CEF overweight RMBS and CMBS bonds. The fund has a low duration profile, which means the managers are able to be agile when it comes to risk-off periods. The recent rise in rates due to the Iran war favors the name via the sell-off seen in mortgage bonds. We like this fund here due to its rates focus and lack of corporate credit exposure.

data.title: S&P 500 To 7,000 And Nasdaq 100 Points To ATH: Are Markets Getting Ahead Of Themselves?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

After remaining close to unchanged in the beginning of the session, stock markets got off to the burners. With no pity for whoever remained short of the war, bulls are leading an absolute rampage, which has now taken two out of the three major US benchmarks just a few points short of their all-time highs.

Traders are still repricing the immense progress in the geopolitical situation, and, from what it seems, the narrative has shifted from global recessions and rate hikes due to oil supply shortages to the US outperforming everybody else yet again.

The end of the Middle Eastern war is certainly good news. With U.S. crude oil orders projected to reach record highs next month and the Strait of Hormuz still in a deadlock, it makes sense for Wall Street to be excited.

Nonetheless, this rally seems to be surpassing everyone's expectations, and not surprisingly, given that a proper peace deal still hasn't been drawn up. At least, the Fintwit arena seems to be lagging behind the move.

A second round of US-Iran negotiations is supposed to take place on Thursday, and with stock markets only focusing on President Trump's words, the one thing they are salivating over is his obsession with reaching a deal illico presto.

The prediction-markets-based odds for a peace deal by April 30 remain unchanged from yesterday (~37%). WTI, on the other hand, is holding steady just above $90 as energy traders remain more skeptical of the potential for a diplomatic solution.

As long as Hormuz remains stuck, the situation for oil prices doesn't change, and the US wins, with a large number of empty tankers now heading to the Gulf of "America" to make Black Gold flow again.

In other news, the Beige Book was just released, and the economic situation that seemed to worsen since October really now seems to be a mere correction, with 8 out of 12 districts reporting modest growth despite the huge raises in energy prices.

The details are showing a bit more internal weakness, but in terms of macroeconomic trends, the activity is just slightly tilting upwards – enough for stock markets to keep up their ecstatic advances.

Will the huge advances continue? As the Dow Jones actually pulls back, the rally isn't as widespread as it seems, and the more tech-focused Nasdaq and S&P 500 are benefiting from large advances in mega caps.

The S&P 500 is now breaking its all-time highs, trading above 7,000 for the first time since January, while the Nasdaq is only a few points away from its October record.

The most pessimistic traders are hoping to see a case of buying the rumor, selling the news, something that could be interesting, but fading such bullish candles could be quite dangerous.

Let's look at intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq Composite, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

The market really is pushing higher from the pursued extension from mega caps, with Microsoft (MSFT) pushing higher by 5.80%, Tesla (TSLA) up 7.40% and the general technology services sub-sector shining bright. On the other hand, the more defensive manufacturing sectors are taking a generalized hit along with utilities and healthcare.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow is now lagging its peers quite remarkably, struggling from intra-market sector dynamics, and failing to surpass its previous session highs.

Once again, the DJIA remains the index to trade for those tracking live sentiment, as the younger benchmarks tend to see exaggeration in tech movement. Looking at the price action, Dow bulls are officially waiting for further news.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Mini resistance: 48,700
  • Major resistance – 49,000 to 49,200
  • 49,500 psychological mini resistance
  • 49,900 to 50,000 resistance and daily range highs

Support Levels

  • Momentum pivot: 48,300 (bull above)
  • Pivotal support at 48,000 (bearish below)
  • Mini support: 47,400 to 47,600
  • War resistance now key support 47,000 +/- 100 points (bearish below)
  • January 2025 highs: 45,000 to 45,280

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq is up close to 15% in a quite insane price action, closing in by the minute to its October all-time highs. The CFD record is at 26,280, while the actual index record is at 26,182 (only 20 points from here).

It wouldn't be surprising to see the record break after such a significant run, but traders could also see high volatility in case the talks fail.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • All-time high resistance: 26,200 to 26,300 (testing)
  • October all-time highs: 26,283
  • Potential resistance: 26,600 to 26,750

Support Levels

  • Momentum pivot: 25,700 to 25,850
  • 25,400 to 25,500 Feb range pivotal support
  • Support 25,000 to 25,250
  • 24,450 to 24,550 key support

S&P 500 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is officially going parabolic, breaking its January all-time high with ease, and aims to officially continue its price discovery. Similarly to Nasdaq, the rally shall continue if the ceasefire talks tomorrow maintain the hopes for a proper peace deal.

Look at 7,050-7,060 for some mild profit-taking in that scenario

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • ATH resistance and range highs from 7,000 to 7,020 (broken)
  • Daily highs: 7,027
  • Next key potential resistance: 7,060 to 7,080

Support Levels

  • December ATH pivot 6,945 to 6,975 (testing)
  • Support 6,880 to 6,900
  • Pivotal support 6,750 to 6,770
  • 6,680 to 6,700 key support
  • 6,300 psychological level (war lows)

Keep track of WTI Crude and the latest headlines throughout the week to stay ahead of the game.

Safe trades!

Original Post
